claim: Survival mode is physiologically driven by acute stress causing serotonin depletion, which impairs short-term memory — explaining why lost dogs fail to recognize their owner or respond to trained commands.
stance_target: Neurological mechanism behind owner non-recognition in survival-mode dogs
scope_conditions: Proposed mechanism; note this is a lay popularization — the serotonin claim is not sourced to peer-reviewed literature
quoted_support: Serotonin is a hormone responsible for a number of bodily functions including depression, sleep, sensory perception and short-term memory. When serotonin is suddenly depleted, i.e., due to stress, short-term memory is compromised. A-ha! Stress and the resulting short-term memory loss is the perfect storm for your dog to go into survival mode.

claim: Onset of survival mode is highly variable — some dogs enter it almost immediately, others not for a week or more — depending on temperament, breed, past experiences, and circumstances.
stance_target: Non-binary survival mode onset model; no fixed time threshold for phase transition
quoted_support: Some dogs go into survival mode almost immediately when separated from their owner. Some do not go into survival mode for a week or more. It depends on the dog, his past experiences, his breed, and the circumstances surrounding him being lost.

claim: Once a survival-mode dog is captured, it returns to its normal personality with very little lasting change — the transformation is temporary and reversible.
stance_target: Post-recovery prognosis; allays owner fear that feral behavior is permanent
scope_conditions: Dogs recovered after survival mode period
quoted_support: But do not fear. Survival mode is only temporary. Once your dog is captured, he will return to normal with very little change in personality.

claim: Cage traps set where the dog was last sighted should be monitored at least every 2 hours in extreme weather conditions to prevent injury or other animals being trapped.
scope_conditions: Extreme cold or hot weather conditions
quoted_support: Once in place, the trap should be checked often, at least every 2 hours in very cold or very hot weather, and to make sure another animal has not been trapped instead.
